topic: Policy-intervention commutator phase for longitudinal causal effects

open_problems.open_problem: Construct a finite two-time binary transition-kernel witness for noncommutation between an odds-multiplier IPSI and a natural-value longitudinal modified treatment policy, with both ordered estimands identified but unequal.

source_refs.claim: Kennedy defines incremental propensity-score interventions as odds-scale shifts of the observed treatment mechanism, with longitudinal identification that weakens standard positivity requirements.
source_refs.author: Kennedy
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: Diaz, Williams, Hoffman, and Schenck identify longitudinal modified treatment policies whose interventions depend on natural treatment values under prior assigned histories.
source_refs.author: Diaz, Williams, Hoffman, and Schenck
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.excerpt: Diaz, I. (2023). Non-parametric causal effects based on longitudinal modified treatment policies.
support-span iff reduces to unfolding tau_j and L_j plus finite-dimensional orthogonal projection

open_problems.why_unsolved: The IPSI and LMTP papers give identification and efficiency theory for each support-preserving intervention class separately, while the prior compositional-reallocation failure shows that rule-dependent intervention effects can collapse to definitional support geometry unless the order of the rule is made operational. The missing object is an operator comparison on one longitudinal law: the literature has no finite witness proving that the odds-law shift and the natural-value map can be individually identified yet fail to commute.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to equate both orders by treating both as stochastic interventions fails because the LMTP natural value at a later time is defined relative to prior assigned intervention history while the IPSI changes the treatment law generated along that history.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A two-time binary model with explicit kernels for L2 given A1, A2 given A1 and L2, and Y given A1, L2, and A2, plus closed formulas for psi_IPSI_after_LMTP and psi_LMTP_after_IPSI whose difference is nonzero on an open set.

open_problems.open_problem: Prove a sharp zero-set theorem for the IPSI-LMTP commutator: the commutator vanishes exactly in the no treatment-confounder-feedback or no effect-modification phase, not merely under broad null-effect conditions.

source_refs.claim: Young, Hernan, and Robins formalize interventions depending on natural treatment values and separate their identification conditions from ordinary dynamic-regime conditions.
source_refs.author: Young, Hernan, and Robins

source_refs.claim: Wen, Marcus, and Young show that intervention distributions depending on the observed treatment process can require representation-specific derivations and need not inherit standard robustness properties.
source_refs.author: Wen, Marcus, and Young

source_refs.excerpt: shifted KKT row-commutator frontier (NOT YET PROVED)
main open work is proving the tangent equivalence and guarding rank-change boundary cases

open_problems.why_unsolved: Natural-value intervention papers name the extra counterfactual machinery needed for such regimes, and Wen-Marcus-Young name representation sensitivity for observed-treatment-process interventions, but neither literature isolates the algebraic phase boundary where opposite policy orders become equal. The prior proximal angle is a directly analogous abandoned commutator-zero-set problem, with rank and boundary cases flagged as the hard part.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to use a no-average-effect null fails because the commutator is an interaction of transition dynamics and outcome contrasts, so marginal equality can occur accidentally without identifying the structural feedback or effect-modification zero set.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A necessary-and-sufficient polynomial factorization for a saturated two-time binary longitudinal law, with interpretable factors corresponding to A1-to-L2 feedback and L2-modification of the A2-to-Y contrast.

open_problems.open_problem: Give paired identification criteria for IPSI-after-LMTP and LMTP-after-IPSI in the same two-time binary observed-data law, including whether the two orders require identical or different counterfactual independences.

source_refs.claim: Diaz, Williams, Hoffman, and Schenck give a sequential regression formula for longitudinal modified treatment policies and distinguish natural-value policies from standard dynamic interventions.
source_refs.author: Diaz, Williams, Hoffman, and Schenck
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: Sarvet, Wanis, Young, Hernandez-Alejandro, and Stensrud define longitudinal limited-resource IPSIs and distinguish natural values from assigned treatment values under regimes.
source_refs.author: Sarvet, Wanis, Young, Hernandez-Alejandro, and Stensrud

source_refs.excerpt: second-stage imperfect compliance may introduce cross-history compatibility constraints
finite-window frontier for sharp reversible-spell bounds (NOT YET PROVED)

open_problems.why_unsolved: The published literature supplies identification criteria for IPSIs, LMTPs, resource-constrained IPSIs, and natural-value interventions, but it does not compare opposite ordered compositions inside one finite law. Prior proposal artifacts separately flag cross-history compatibility and finite-window transition-kernel frontiers, which are exactly the obstacles that an ordered-composition criterion must expose rather than assume away.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to invoke standard longitudinal exchangeability fails because one ordered policy may require the distribution of a natural treatment value under a prior stochastic shift, whereas the other conditions on a different assigned-history world.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: Two SWIG-style or extended-g-formula identification statements for the opposite ordered estimands, followed by a finite binary example where both are identified from observed kernels yet differ.

open_problems.open_problem: Define a representation-invariant equivalence relation for stochastic-policy operators so the IPSI-LMTP commutator is about causal regimes rather than alternative representations of the same observed-data g-formula functional.

source_refs.claim: van der Laan, Luedtke, and Diaz emphasize that distinct causal questions can lead to the same statistical estimand, requiring careful separation of causal and statistical interpretations.
source_refs.author: van der Laan, Luedtke, and Diaz

source_refs.claim: Wen, Marcus, and Young note that multiplicative-shift intervention distributions can have multiple modified-treatment-policy representations yielding the same functional.
source_refs.author: Wen, Marcus, and Young

open_problems.why_unsolved: The statistical literature explicitly warns that one observed-data estimand can correspond to more than one causal interpretation, and Wen-Marcus-Young show that multiplicative shifts can be routed through alternative stochastic or modified-policy constructions. What is missing is a criterion saying which representation is fixed before composing with a natural-value policy, or whether the commutator can be made invariant to that choice.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to compare only observed-data functionals fails because two causally distinct ordered regimes can map to the same formula in one special representation but diverge once embedded in a second intervention.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A definition of policy-operator equivalence under which the IPSI-LMTP commutator is invariant, or a counterexample proving that no such invariant exists without fixing natural-value semantics.

open_problems.open_problem: Extend the commutator witness to constrained IPSIs where the odds or resource multiplier is law-dependent and chosen to meet a marginal treatment-utilization cap.

source_refs.claim: Sarvet, Wanis, Young, Hernandez-Alejandro, and Stensrud introduce limited-resource IPSIs that proportionally scale propensities, control marginal utilization, and preserve propensity rank ordering.
source_refs.author: Sarvet, Wanis, Young, Hernandez-Alejandro, and Stensrud

source_refs.claim: Young, Logan, Robins, and Hernan develop representative random dynamic interventions for time-varying treatments as realistic stochastic alternatives to deterministic policies.
source_refs.author: Young, Logan, Robins, and Hernan
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

open_problems.why_unsolved: Resource-constrained IPSIs handle realistic allocation limits by preserving natural propensity ordering and solving for scaling factors, while representative interventions handle realistic stochastic longitudinal regimes. Neither line studies what happens when a natural-value LMTP is applied before or after the law-dependent resource scaling, where the feasible allocation surface itself can change with order.
open_problems.why_nontrivial: The natural attempt to plug the resource multiplier into the ordinary IPSI commutator fails because the multiplier is itself a functional of the post-policy distribution, so changing order changes the constraint equation.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A binary two-time resource-cap model with solved scaling factors in both orders, an explicit commutator difference formula, and conditions under which resource constraints amplify, dampen, or erase the base commutator.

literature_map:
Kennedy 2019 JASA defines IPSIs as odds-multiplier stochastic interventions, giving longitudinal causal effects that avoid standard deterministic-intervention positivity demands.
Diaz and van der Laan 2012 Biometrics establishes population intervention causal effects for stochastic interventions, the broader statistical ancestor of this line.
Haneuse and Rotnitzky 2013 Statistics in Medicine introduces modified treatment policies by modifying the received or natural exposure, mainly for point-treatment settings.
Young, Hernan, and Robins 2014 Epidemiologic Methods formalizes interventions depending on natural treatment values and separates their identification assumptions from ordinary dynamic regimes.
van der Laan, Luedtke, and Diaz 2014 Epidemiologic Methods emphasizes that different causal questions can share one statistical estimand, which matters for operator-composition claims.
Young, Logan, Robins, and Hernan 2019 JASA studies representative random dynamic interventions, another realistic-policy route to stochastic longitudinal g-formulas.
Diaz, Williams, Hoffman, and Schenck 2023 JASA gives longitudinal modified treatment policies, sequential-regression identification, and an LMTP stochastic-intervention variant.
Wen, Marcus, and Young 2023 Statistical Methods in Medical Research shows that intervention distributions depending on the observed treatment process can have representation-sensitive robustness.
Sarvet, Wanis, Young, Hernandez-Alejandro, and Stensrud 2023 Biometrics develops resource-constrained IPSIs and distinguishes natural and assigned treatment values while preserving propensity rank ordering.
Hoffman, Salazar-Barreto, Williams, Rudolph, and Diaz 2024 Epidemiology broadens LMTPs to binary, multivariate, continuous, time-varying, and complex exposures, making a binary commutator witness plausible.
Stensrud, Laurendeau, and Sarvet 2024 Biometrika treats natural treatment intention as usable decision information and highlights deployment shifts in the natural-treatment distribution.
The live tension is that support-preserving IPSIs and support-preserving natural-value policies are each identified, but no paper gives a finite transition-kernel algebra, commutator coefficient, or exact zero-phase theorem for their ordered composition.
prior_proposal_map:
No completed prior proposal for eid_policy_commutator_phase was present; only research state/pipeline files appeared.
pid_policy_regret_ch_sensitivity_v1 is the closest policy-proposal material: scalar bridges among CH, MSM odds-ratio, and covariate-shift policy criteria, with unresolved finite boundary witnesses.
pid_offline_policy_joint_misspec_v1 supplies a non-rectangularity warning: composing sharp one-source policy bounds can fail when constraints share latent response types.
pid_reversible_dynamic_spillover_v1 is the closest longitudinal g-formula/transition-kernel bank entry, but it was downgraded after the universal nested g-formula bridge was withdrawn.
pid_dynamic_iv_compliance_v2 adds evidence that prior history or assignment memory can be identification-relevant and should not be silently Markovized.
eid_compositional_reallocation_effect_v1 is the closest modified-treatment-policy rule-dependence artifact and explicitly cites Diaz 2023 LMTP, but its prior kernel collapsed to support-span geometry.
q_did_drdid_ipwdid_frontier_v1 gives a cautionary template for zero frontiers: do not assume the bridge or algorithm whose zero set must be proved.
eid_proximal_completeness_v1 angle2 is not policy-focused but provides the clearest prior commutator-zero-set language and open witness pattern.
The supplied Stage 6 study-pipeline gaps were reviewed as augmenting candidates, but they concern LATE, Manski bounds, and interval-regression inference rather than the policy-commutator topic.
